data.title: Dow anticipates $12B Q2 revenue and $2B EBITDA amid Middle East-driven supply disruption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dow Inc. (DOW) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered 3% sequential volume growth, net sales of $9.8 billion and operating EBITDA of $873 million" (Chairman & CEO James Fitterling), while adding that March demand inflected sharply as "the beginning of the conflict in the Middle East" triggered supply dislocation.

  • "We expect this supply disruption will persist throughout 2026" (Chairman & CEO Fitterling), alongside expectations that "the pricing momentum that began in March" continues "across every business and every region".

  • "Effective July 1, Karen will assume the role of Chief Executive Officer, and I will move to the role of Executive Chair" (Chairman & CEO Fitterling), describing it as following "a deliberate multiyear succession process".

  • "Our operations outside the region experienced the largest percent sales gain from February to March that we've seen in our company's history" (Chief Operating Officer Karen Carter), as the company emphasized cost and cash control while prioritizing customers.

  • Segment details included: "First quarter net sales were $4.9 billion" in Packaging and Specialty Plastics and "Operating EBIT was $208 million" (Chief Operating Officer Carter); "Net sales were $2.6 billion, down 8% year-over-year" in Industrial Intermediates & Infrastructure (Chief Operating Officer Carter); and "Net sales were $2.1 billion" in Performance Materials & Coatings, with "Volume increased 2% year-over-year" (Chief Operating Officer Carter).

  • "Our expectation for second quarter are approximately $12 billion of revenue and EBITDA of $2 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Jeffrey Tate), citing pricing gains, expanding margins, asset utilization, seasonal demand and cost reductions.

Outlook

  • "Our expectation for second quarter are approximately $12 billion of revenue and EBITDA of $2 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Tate), and he said the sequential improvement is expected to "more than offset rising feedstock and energy costs, plant maintenance activity and expected sequential decreases in licensing revenue".

  • "Beginning this quarter, we suspended Sadara equity loss recognition in accordance with U.S. GAAP" (Chief Financial Officer Tate), explaining it occurred after "the carrying value of all liabilities on the balance sheet reached a total of Dow's existing relevant obligations and commitments".

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, Dow’s modeling guidance moved from "first quarter EBITDA is approximately $750 million" (Chief Financial Officer Tate, Q4 2025 call) to the Q2 2026 modeling range above, with management tying the change to Middle East supply disruption and pricing momentum.

Financial Results

  • "We delivered 3% sequential volume growth, net sales of $9.8 billion and operating EBITDA of $873 million" (Chairman & CEO Fitterling).

  • "We delivered approximately $193 million in period cost savings" (Chairman & CEO Fitterling).

  • Liquidity and cash management highlights included: "we saw a year-over-year improvement in working capital of greater than $300 million" (Chief Financial Officer Tate); "We have approximately $14 billion of total liquidity" (Chief Financial Officer Tate); and "We also ended first quarter with over $4 billion of cash on hand" (Chief Financial Officer Tate).

Q&A

  • Hassan Ahmed, Alembic Global Advisors: Question on supply-chain normalization timing and polyethylene pricing sustainability; Chairman & CEO Fitterling: "we did some modeling at that time that it would be 275 days or longer for the supply chain disruption to unwind" and "we don't have any sign in place that the Strait are going to reopen"; Chief Operating Officer Carter: "we believe that there's more room for prices to move up" and said that could be "upside to the $2 billion guide".

  • Michael Sison, Wells Fargo: Question framing Packaging and Specialty Plastics Q2 EBITDA level and durability; Chief Operating Officer Carter: "That mid-cycle perhaps a bit above mid-cycle" and described "mid-cycle moving to peak levels" while also saying the recovery "is going to take 6 months anywhere to 18 months to resolve".

  • Vincent Andrews, Morgan Stanley: Question on Europe cost curve and near-term profitability; Chairman & CEO Fitterling: "Europe, we returned to profitability" but also "I don't think it changes our long-term outlook on Europe"; Chief Operating Officer Carter: "we anticipate that margins in the second quarter are also going to go up in Europe for us".

  • Jeffrey Zekauskas, JPMorgan: Question on regional polyethylene pricing dislocations and Sadara cash; Chief Operating Officer Carter: "our prices around the world are going up"; Chief Financial Officer Tate: Sadara commitments included "approximately $100 million per year" and obligations comprising "$1.2 billion of debt" plus about "$100 million" revolver and "$100 million" letter of credit.

  • Kevin McCarthy, Vertical Research Partners: Question on durable supply impacts and asset damage; Chairman & CEO Fitterling: the Qatar LNG strike involved "a very critical piece of equipment that takes 2.5 to 3 years to build" while most petrochemical-related repairs were expected within the logistics-unwind window.

  • Frank Mitsch, Fermium Research: Question on Sadara future and accounting; Chairman & CEO Fitterling: "finish up these negotiations with Saudi Aramco on the restructuring of Sadara" and said the issue was "more of a leverage issue and a balance sheet issue"; Chief Financial Officer Tate: "the equity loss impact there was $115 million" and full-year Sadara impact "approximately $400 million".

  • David Begleiter, Deutsche Bank: Question on how much of announced price increases are embedded; Chief Operating Officer Carter: "it includes our April price increase that's on the table, but it does not include May" and "May would present upside to the guide".

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to pressing, with repeated challenges to durability and cash impacts, including questions on "the sustainability of some of these pricing initiatives" (Hassan Ahmed, Alembic) and detailed Sadara cash commitments (Jeffrey Zekauskas, JPMorgan).

  • Management sentiment was positive but conditional on conflict-driven tightness; examples included "we expect more potential upside to these projections than downside" (Chief Financial Officer Tate) and "we believe that there's more room for prices to move up" (Chief Operating Officer Carter).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s tone shifted from transformation/cost-reset emphasis to conflict-driven market tightness and pricing, while still reiterating self-help; in Q4 2025, management introduced "Transform to Outperform" (Chairman & CEO Fitterling, prior call), whereas Q1 2026 leaned on supply disruption persistence and near-term pricing leverage.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from seasonality and planned maintenance ("first quarter EBITDA is approximately $750 million" (Chief Financial Officer Tate, prior call)) to conflict-driven tightness and pricing, with Q1 2026 stating "Our expectation for second quarter are approximately $12 billion of revenue and EBITDA of $2 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Tate).

  • Strategic focus expanded from launching "Transform to Outperform" and Path2Zero timeline updates (prior call) to a major leadership transition ("Effective July 1, Karen will assume the role of Chief Executive Officer" (Chairman & CEO Fitterling)) and conflict-linked supply-chain disruption expectations.

  • Analyst focus moved from structural oversupply/rationalization and Alberta project financing (prior call) toward the duration of Strait-of-Hormuz disruptions, polyethylene pricing sustainability, and Sadara cash and accounting treatment.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The situation in the Middle East has introduced volatility and uncertainty" (Chief Financial Officer Tate), including "a headwind from the safe proactive shutdown of our facilities in Kuwait" and "Lower feedstock availability at our Thailand joint ventures".

  • Management highlighted disruption scale: "Roughly 20% of global oil capacity is currently offline" and "approximately half of global ethylene and polyethylene supply is either offline, constrained or directly impacted" (Chief Operating Officer Carter).

  • Sadara remained a balance-sheet and cash-commitment overhang in investor questioning; management cited restructuring efforts and stated future cash commitments were "approximately $100 million per year" (Chief Financial Officer Tate).

Final Takeaway

Dow’s management framed Q1 2026 as a pivot from early-quarter stability to March-driven tightness tied to Middle East supply disruptions, pairing the shift with a modeling outlook of approximately $12 billion in Q2 revenue and $2 billion in EBITDA. Executives emphasized accelerating global polyethylene pricing actions (with May not included in guidance), near-term margin uplift opportunities in Europe, and continued cost/self-help programs, while also announcing a CEO transition effective July 1. In Q&A, analysts repeatedly pressed on how durable the disruption and pricing would be, and management pointed to an extended logistics unwind timeline, ongoing constraints, and identified upside potential relative to its Q2 modeling assumptions.

data.title: Rollins expects 7% to 8% organic growth in 2026 while keeping M&A at 2% to 3%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rollins, Inc. (ROL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Rollins highlighted accelerating demand exiting the quarter, with CEO Jerry Gahlhoff reporting, "Rollins delivered strong first quarter results" and adding, "we exited with well over 8% organic growth in March." He also said, "we continued our investments in incremental sales staffing and marketing activities ahead of peak season" and noted staffing readiness: "We are well staffed on the sales, technician and customer support front."
  • Management emphasized M&A as an active growth lever, with CEO Gahlhoff stating, "Earlier this month, we announced our acquisition of Romex Pest Control" and calling it "another example of our successful M&A playbook in action." He said the deal "provides us with entry points into new markets" and that Romex has "a strong people and customer-focused culture."
  • Commercial momentum was positioned as improving, with CEO Gahlhoff saying, "we're encouraged by our momentum" and adding, "These resources are paying off as Orkin Commercial continues to deliver new customer wins across key verticals."
  • Profitability headwinds were framed as timing and cost-related, with CEO Gahlhoff stating, "we saw headwinds to profitability from higher insurance and claims" and "some pressure from headcount given lower volume earlier in the quarter," while defending pre-peak staffing: "This hinders profitability in the short term, but is the right decision for the business long term."
  • CFO Ken Krause summarized the quarter’s financial cadence, saying, "In total, we delivered revenue growth of 10.2% year-over-year" and "Organic growth of 6.6%... represents 90 basis points of improvement versus Q4 of 2025," while pointing to service-line performance: "Residential revenues increased 9.3%, commercial pest control rose 9.6% and termite and ancillary increased by 13.5%."

Outlook

  • Rollins reiterated its full-year growth framework, with CFO Krause stating, "We continue to expect organic growth in the 7% to 8% range for the year with growth from M&A of 2% to 3%." He also said, "We currently expect fuel cost to continue to track below 2% of sales in 2026" and "we anticipate that cash flow will continue to convert at a rate that is above 100% in 2026."
  • Pricing expectations were specified as a growth contributor, with CFO Krause saying, "We are seeing good receptivity on our recent price increase" and "expect price to contribute 3% to 4% of growth for the year ahead of CPI," adding, "we expect to be positive on price/cost for the year at that level of price realization."
  • Versus the prior quarter’s call, the organic-growth and M&A targets were reiterated in the same ranges; in Q4, CFO Krause said, "We continue to expect organic growth in the range of 7% to 8% with additional growth from M&A of at least 2% to 3%," and Q1 repeated that outlook while emphasizing March acceleration.

Financial Results

  • Revenue and margins reflected early-quarter weather and cost headwinds, with CFO Krause stating, "Organic growth of 6.6% was negatively impacted by unfavorable weather, particularly in January," and reporting, "Our gross margins were 50.8%, a decrease of 60 basis points." He attributed gross margin pressure to, "lower vehicle gains... created 50 basis points of headwind," plus "Insurance and claims drove an additional 30 basis points" and "service payroll costs provided 20 basis points of headwinds."
  • Operating profitability and earnings were detailed by management, with CFO Krause reporting, "First quarter GAAP operating income was $145 million," "Adjusted operating income was $153 million," and "First quarter adjusted EBITDA was $179 million... a 19.8% margin." He added, "Quarterly GAAP net income was $108 million or $0.22 per share" and "adjusted net income... was $113 million or $0.24 per share."
  • Cash flow and capital allocation were described with specific figures, with CFO Krause saying, "We delivered operating cash flow of $118 million and free cash flow of $111 million," and adding, "During the first quarter, we made acquisitions totaling $18 million, and we paid $88 million in dividends" and "Our leverage ratio stands at 0.9x."

Q&A

  • John Ronan Kennedy, Barclays Bank PLC: Question on March exit-rate sustainability and whether it was normalization vs. stronger demand; Executive VP & CFO Krause: "We feel good about the exit rate" and tied it to guidance, saying, "Our outlook is rooted in that 7% to 8% organic growth," adding March reflected "really good demand" and noting residential improved to "over 7%" in March.
  • John Ronan Kennedy, Barclays Bank PLC: Question on incremental margin flow-through as volume ramps; Executive VP & CFO Krause: "Q1 is usually our low point" and "We fully expect improvements... as we ramp into Q2 and Q3."
  • Samuel Kusswurm, William Blair: Question on April trend vs. March; Executive VP & CFO Krause: "We're early... in April" and reiterated, "we still have a lot of confidence in that 7% to 8% organic growth."
  • Samuel Kusswurm, William Blair: Question on elevated insurance and claims; Executive VP & CFO Krause: "That's a hard one to predict" and "We're hopeful that we'll see it moderate," while maintaining, "we are still holding strong to our incremental margin profile" and "our ability to grow earnings in that double-digit range." CEO Gahlhoff added mitigation language: "We're piloting a lot of programs, making investments, especially in driving safety."
  • Gregory Parrish, Morgan Stanley: Question on Romex rationale; CEO Gahlhoff: "We've got some really talented people" and cited "complementary markets" plus cross-selling potential to "add additional services to customers."
  • Tomohiko Sano, JPMorgan: Question on March by segment/region; Executive VP & CFO Krause: "the business is very healthy in March" and said residential "showed the greatest improvement" while "Commercial also was stronger" and "termite and ancillary hung in there."
  • Curtis Nagle, BofA Securities: Question on recurring vs. onetime; Executive VP & CFO Krause: recurring was "very healthy" exiting March and onetime "continued to accelerate" with "a pretty healthy backlog."
  • Peter Keith, Piper Sandler: Question on cash flow timing headwinds; Executive VP & CFO Krause: "The interest expense certainly will" reverse by timing, and for taxes, "you won't see it reach its full potential until Q4," while targeting "that mid-teens sort of growth rate in cash."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral to slightly positive, with repeated probing on whether March strength was sustainable and whether insurance/claims would remain elevated (e.g., Barclays asking about "sustainability" and William Blair highlighting insurance/claims at "3.7% of sales").
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, repeatedly returning to confidence in guidance (Executive VP & CFO Krause: "We remain committed to that level of growth") while acknowledging uncertainty in insurance/claims ("hard one to predict") and emphasizing improvement into Q2/Q3 ("fully expect improvements").
  • Versus Q4, management’s tone shifted from weather-related caution about a "tougher finish" (CEO Gahlhoff in Q4) to stronger emphasis on acceleration and exit-rate momentum in Q1 (CEO Gahlhoff: "sequential acceleration through the quarter").

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 messaging centered on improving trends through the quarter, while Q4 centered on weather-driven softness in onetime work; in Q1, CEO Gahlhoff said, "we exited with well over 8% organic growth in March," whereas in Q4 he emphasized, "Early winter weather caused demand to soften" and onetime revenue "declined by almost 3%."
  • Both quarters reiterated the same core full-year framework for 2026, with Q1 CFO Krause repeating "organic growth in the 7% to 8% range" and "M&A... 2% to 3%," consistent with Q4 guidance language.
  • Q1 introduced a new announced acquisition (Romex) as a headline development, while Q4 focused on Saela performance and broader tuck-in activity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited weather volatility as a recurring operational risk, with CFO Krause noting Q1 organic growth was "negatively impacted by unfavorable weather, particularly in January."
  • Insurance and claims were described as volatile and potentially multi-year in impact, with CFO Krause stating, "it's really hard to predict what that line will look like" and later adding some claims "are 3, 4, 5 years old," while also saying the company is seeing "great improvements in our safety experience" as a leading indicator.
  • Near-term margin pressure from staffing ahead of peak season was acknowledged, with CEO Gahlhoff stating, "we maintain healthy staffing levels ahead of peak season" even if it "hinders profitability in the short term."

Final Takeaway

Rollins’ management emphasized a stronger demand trajectory exiting Q1, anchored by March performance, while reiterating full-year expectations for 7% to 8% organic growth plus 2% to 3% from M&A. The company highlighted the Romex acquisition, ongoing commercial investments, and pricing actions expected to contribute 3% to 4% of growth, while acknowledging margin headwinds from lower vehicle gains, elevated insurance and claims, and pre-peak staffing decisions that management characterized as deliberate to protect service quality and capture peak-season demand.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Google facing EU pressure to open Android to Gemini rivals -- Bloomberg
data.description:
As part of its Digital Markets Act, the European Union is expected to pressure Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) to allow rival AI search engines access to Android’s operating system, features that are currently only available to Google’s (GOOG) Gemini AI assistant. 

However, Google (GOOG) argues that by removing any security protocols to allow access to AI tools like OpenAI’s (OPENAI) ChatGPT or Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) Claude, Android users’ privacy and security would be compromised.

According to sources cited by Bloomberg, EU watchdogs are set to force Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) to comply with its Digital Markets Act by ”specifying how rival AI services should be granted an equally effective level of access to Android features.”

Should Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) resist the EU’s request, a formal probe—which would include financial penalties—could be launched against the company.

data.title: HR chiefs signal expectations for more hiring ahead , The Conference Board says
data.description:
The Conference Board's U.S. CHRO (Chief Human Resource Officer) Confidence Index hit a new high of 59 in Q1 2026, the strongest reading since the series began three years ago, the organization said on Thursday. A reading over 50 reflects more positive than negative responses. The gauge improved from 57 in Q4 2025.

The increase was primarily driven by hiring expectations and rising confidence in employee engagement. The hiring component of the index advanced to 63 in Q1 from 60 in Q4 2025. Of the 114 CHROs surveyed, 59% expect to increase hiring in the first half of 2026, up from 54% in Q4 2025.

Employee engagement expectations jumped to 50 from 56; 53% of respondents expect engagement levels to increase, up from 43% in Q4. On the flip side, 21% expect engagement levels to decline, down from 24% in the previous quarter.

The index's retention component edged up to 55 from 53, with 34% of the respondents expecting improvement in retention, up from 31% in the prior quarter. Almost half of the respondents expected no change.

"Hiring momentum is back, but retention is where the real work begins. CHROs should treat this moment as an opportunity to redesign the employee experience, investing in leadership quality, skills development, and more personalized employee engagement," said Diana Scott, U.S. Human Capital Center leader at The Conference Board.

data.title: Tesla after earnings: What do technicals now signal for shares?
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) shares were under pressure Thursday after the company’s Q1 earnings report, with investors now focused on whether key technical levels can hold following the post-results selloff.

At press time, shares were recently trading at $376, down 2.9%, and remain below the 50-day moving average of $389.15 and the 200-day moving average of $400.07, signaling weakened near-term momentum.

Before earnings, Tesla’s weekly options had implied an approximately 6% move, and shares are now trading near the lower half of that expected range ($390 to $400), suggesting investors were underwhelmed by the results.

The electric vehicle company shows a put/call open interest ratio of 0.77, with total put open interest at 2.87M compared to call open interest of 3.74M.

On the upside, traders are likely watching the $389-$390 zone as the first resistance area, which aligns with the 50-day moving average and marks a key near-term recovery level. A sustained move above that range could improve sentiment and open the door toward $400, where the 200-day moving average now stands.

"Tesla is expected to be well positioned for another leg up towards a price target of $415 per share based on its current roadmap and anticipated execution progress," SA columnist Summit Research said.

"Tesla, Inc.'s Q1 double beat reinforces the view that the company can still generate resilient earnings while funding a more capital-intensive AI, autonomy and robotics roadmap."
